title: Prompt Injection at Work: Real-World Risks

modules.title: Beyond the Chatbot: Where Prompt Injection Hides in Everyday Professional Tools
modules.sub: Mapping the hidden attack surface in AI-augmented workplaces
modules.description: Learners survey the landscape of AI-assisted tools embedded in modern professional workflows and identify which ones create prompt injection exposure. By the end of this module, learners can name specific tool categories in their own environment that carry injection risk they may not have previously considered.

modules.title: Attack Scenarios: Email Assistants, Document Summarizers, CRM Copilots, and HR Bots
modules.sub: Seeing prompt injection play out across four common workplace tools
modules.description: Learners walk through realistic attack scenarios staged across email assistants, document summarizers, CRM copilots, and HR bots, analyzing how malicious instructions enter each system. They practice distinguishing legitimate AI behavior from behavior that has been redirected by injected content embedded in external inputs.

modules.title: How Injected Instructions Exfiltrate Data, Manipulate Outputs, and Escalate Privileges
modules.sub: Understanding the mechanics and consequences of a successful injection
modules.description: Learners examine the technical pathways through which injected prompts cause real harm, including data leakage, fabricated outputs, and unauthorized privilege escalation. They analyze case-based examples to connect attack mechanics to tangible business and security consequences, building the vocabulary needed to communicate risk to technical and non-technical stakeholders.

modules.title: Recognizing Suspicious AI Behavior: Warning Signs Any User Can Spot
modules.sub: Giving every employee a practical detection checklist
modules.description: Learners develop a personal checklist of observable warning signs that may indicate an AI tool's behavior has been manipulated, such as unexpected tone shifts, unsolicited data requests, or instructions to ignore previous guidance. They apply this checklist to scenario-based exercises, making go or no-go decisions about whether to flag AI output for review.

modules.title: Organizational Controls: Prompt Hardening, Output Filtering, and Tool Permissions
modules.sub: Selecting and prioritizing defensive measures for AI deployments
modules.description: Learners evaluate a set of organizational controls — including prompt hardening techniques, output filtering policies, and least-privilege permission models — and assess which controls apply to specific tool configurations. They produce a prioritized control recommendation for a sample AI deployment, justifying their choices against realistic resource and usability constraints.

modules.title: Building an Incident Response Playbook for AI-Assisted Workflow Compromises
modules.sub: Drafting step-by-step response procedures for AI tool incidents
modules.description: Learners construct a structured incident response playbook tailored to prompt injection events, covering detection triggers, containment steps, evidence preservation, and escalation paths. They adapt a provided template to fit a realistic organizational scenario, making decisions about roles, communication protocols, and remediation sequencing.

modules.title: Training Your Workforce: Practical Exercises for Security and Non-Security Staff
modules.sub: Designing role-appropriate awareness activities employees will actually use
modules.description: Learners design and evaluate training exercises suited to both security professionals and general staff, accounting for differences in technical background and daily tool exposure. They select or adapt scenario-based activities — such as tabletop drills and spot-the-injection challenges — and identify metrics for measuring workforce readiness over time.

synopsis: Prompt injection is no longer a theoretical research curiosity — it is actively exploited through AI-assisted email, document processing, and enterprise copilot tools that millions of professionals use every day, often without any awareness that the threat exists. This course translates the attack mechanics into concrete workplace scenarios and gives both everyday users and security teams the language and actions they need to recognize, report, and reduce exposure.
rationale: Enterprise AI tool adoption has far outpaced workforce awareness of prompt injection risk, creating a wide-open social engineering surface that existing security awareness programs almost universally fail to address.
learning_outcome: Learners can identify prompt injection risk in the AI-assisted tools they use daily, apply basic input and output hygiene practices, and contribute to or implement an organizational response plan when AI tool behavior appears manipulated.
source_signals: Anti-recruiter prompt injection attack in LinkedIn profile
AI-powered Bing Chat spills its secrets via prompt injection attack
